skills.skill: Midpoints - determine the coordinates of a point that is a given fractional distance less than one from one end of a line segment to the other in one- and two-dimensional coordinate systems, including finding the midpoint;
skills.prompt: This topic covers midpoints (determine the coordinates of a point that is a given fractional distance less than one from one end of a line segment to the other in one- and two-dimensional coordinate systems, including finding the midpoint;) for Geometry students (TEKS G.2(A)). The midpoint formula M = ((x1+x2)/2, (y1+y2)/2) finds the point exactly halfway between two endpoints. The distance formula d = sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2) derives from the Pythagorean theorem. Key vocabulary includes: Pythagorean theorem, average, coordinates, distance formula, length, midpoint, segment. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Geometry. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: Slope, Distance, Midpoint - derive and use the distance, slope, and midpoint formulas to verify geometric relationships, including congruence of segments and parallelism or perpendicularity of pairs of lines;
skills.prompt: This topic covers slope, distance, midpoint (derive and use the distance, slope, and midpoint formulas to verify geometric relationships, including congruence of segments and parallelism or perpendicularity of pairs of lines;) for Geometry students (TEKS G.2(B)). The midpoint formula M = ((x1+x2)/2, (y1+y2)/2) finds the point exactly halfway between two endpoints. The distance formula d = sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2) derives from the Pythagorean theorem. Slope m = (y2-y1)/(x2-x1) measures steepness; parallel lines have equal slopes, perpendicular lines have negative reciprocal slopes. Congruent figures have the same shape and size; corresponding parts of congruent triangles are congruent (CPCTC). Students construct logical proofs using two-column, paragraph, or flow-chart format, citing definitions, postulates, and theorems. Key vocabulary includes: CPCTC, Pythagorean theorem, average, congruent, coordinates, corresponding parts, distance formula, given, length, midpoint, parallel, perpendicular, postulate, proof, rate of change, reason, rigid motion, rise, run, segment, slope, statement, theorem. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Geometry. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: Equations of lines - determine an equation of a line parallel or perpendicular to a given line that passes through a given point.
skills.prompt: This topic covers writing equations of lines parallel or perpendicular to given lines through specified points for Geometry students (TEKS G.2(C)). For parallel: use same slope. For perpendicular: use negative reciprocal slope. Apply point-slope form y-y1=m(x-x1) with the new point. Key vocabulary: parallel, perpendicular, slope, negative reciprocal, point-slope form. Common errors: using wrong slope relationship, algebraic errors in point-slope form, and not converting to required form.

skills.prompt: This topic covers writing equations of lines parallel or perpendicular to given lines through specified points for Geometry students (TEKS G.2(C)). For parallel: use same slope. Common errors: using wrong slope relationship, algebraic errors in point-slope form, and not converting to required form.

skills.skill: Transformations - describe and perform transformations of figures in a plane using coordinate notation;
skills.prompt: This topic covers transformations (describe and perform transformations of figures in a plane using coordinate notation;) for Geometry students (TEKS G.3(A)). Rigid motions (translations, reflections, rotations) preserve distance and angle measure, making pre-image and image congruent. Key vocabulary includes: coordinate notation, image, pre-image, rigid motion, transformation. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Geometry. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: Transformations - Translations: graph the image
skills.prompt: This topic covers transformations (translations: graph the image) for Geometry students (TEKS G.3(A)). Rigid motions (translations, reflections, rotations) preserve distance and angle measure, making pre-image and image congruent. Translations slide every point the same distance and direction: (x,y) -> (x+a, y+b). Key vocabulary includes: coordinate notation, image, pre-image, rigid motion, transformation. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Geometry. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.prompt: This topic covers transformations (reflections: graph the image) for Geometry students (TEKS G.3(A)). Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Geometry. skills.skill: Symmetry - identify and distinguish between reflectional and rotational symmetry in a plane figure.
skills.prompt: This topic covers symmetry (identify and distinguish between reflectional and rotational symmetry in a plane figure.) for Geometry students (TEKS G.3(D)). Rigid motions (translations, reflections, rotations) preserve distance and angle measure, making pre-image and image congruent. Reflections create mirror images across a line: over x-axis (x,-y), over y-axis (-x,y), over y=x (y,x). Rotations turn figures about a point: 90 CCW (-y,x), 180 (-x,-y), 270 CCW (y,-x). A figure has rotational symmetry if it maps onto itself under rotation less than 360 degrees; order = number of such rotations. Key vocabulary includes: axis of symmetry, coordinate notation, image, line symmetry, order, pre-image, rigid motion, rotational symmetry, transformation. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Geometry. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.skill: Symmetry - Line symmetry
skills.prompt: This topic covers symmetry (line symmetry) for Geometry students (TEKS G.3(D)). A figure has line symmetry if a line divides it into two congruent mirror-image halves. Key vocabulary includes: axis of symmetry, line symmetry, order, rotational symmetry. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Geometry. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.

skills.prompt: This topic covers symmetry (rotational symmetry) for Geometry students (TEKS G.3(D)). Rigid motions (translations, reflections, rotations) preserve distance and angle measure, making pre-image and image congruent. Rotations turn figures about a point: 90 CCW (-y,x), 180 (-x,-y), 270 CCW (y,-x). A figure has rotational symmetry if it maps onto itself under rotation less than 360 degrees; order = number of such rotations. Key vocabulary includes: axis of symmetry, coordinate notation, image, line symmetry, order, pre-image, rigid motion, rotational symmetry, transformation. Students should practice with varied problem types, show clear mathematical reasoning, and verify their answers. Connect this topic to real-world applications and prior knowledge from Geometry. Common errors to watch for include computational mistakes, misapplication of definitions or formulas, sign errors, and incomplete reasoning or justification.
